summary: Fowler argues that 'Service Oriented Architecture' has become meaninglessly ambiguous, with the term encompassing so many contradictory definitions that it's impossible to answer questions about it. He catalogs the wildly different interpretations: web services exposure, application-less architectures with UI aggregators, standardized communication backbones, and asynchronous document messaging. While acknowledging that Object Orientation also suffered from definitional debates, he contends SOA's ambiguity is far worse. His conclusion is that the term is beyond saving and the concrete ideas within it need independent names and descriptions.
key_points: SOA means radically different things to different people - from web services to messaging to organizational backbones
The contradictions are extreme: people claim SOA both separates and combines data and process, is both synchronous and asynchronous
While Object Orientation had definitional debates, SOA's ambiguity is far more severe
SOA has become a 'semantics-free concept' like 'components' and 'architecture'
The valuable concrete ideas hiding under SOA need to be named and described independently
money_quotes: It's a question that's pretty much impossible to answer because SOA means so many different things to different people.
In it's worse form this is 'CORBA with angle brackets'.

I've heard people say the nice thing about SOA is that it separates data from process, that it combines data and process, that it uses web standards, that it's independent of web standards, that it's asynchronous, that it's synchronous, that the synchronicity doesn't matter...
I played it for laughs by asking if anyone else understood what on earth SOA was.
There's far less Object Ambiguity than there is Service Oriented Ambiguity.
I think SOA has turned into a semantics-free concept that can join 'components' and 'architecture'. It's beyond saving.
key_insight: When a technical term accumulates so many contradictory meanings that it can simultaneously mean opposite things, it becomes useless and the concrete ideas within it need their own independent terminology.
